comments:
Summary:
This paper proposes a unified view of quantification methods by casting existing approaches as instances of a constrained multivariate regression problem. The author shows how different feature transformations and loss functions correspond to previously proposed methods, provides solution recipes for least-squares, least-absolute-deviation and Hellinger divergence objectives, extends several binary-only approaches to the multi-class case, and reports empirical results on several datasets (Stanford Sentiment Treebank, CIFAR-10, a marketing dataset, and multiple Twitter datasets). Code is made available.

Strengths:
- The constrained-regression formulation provides a clear and useful unifying perspective that helps conceptually organize a range of previously disparate methods.
- Extending binary methods to a principled multivariate setting is practically valuable; the paper gives concrete optimization formulations (QPs, LPs, nonlinear) that are straightforward to implement.
- Experiments cover multiple domains and include both synthetic proportion variations and more realistic random-walk style shifts; use of MAD and post-quantification classification accuracy are reasonable evaluation metrics.
- Code availability increases reproducibility.

Weaknesses and concerns:
- The key assumption that the conditional distribution of the features given class remains identical between training and test is very strong. The paper acknowledges but does not explore robustness when this assumption is violated; empirical sensitivity analyses to distributional shifts in feature distributions are absent.
- Methodological details are sometimes thin: e.g., choices for binning (when using bin()), how many cross-validation repetitions were used, solver/tolerance settings, any regularization or numerical stability measures. For the Hellinger objective the optimization is non-linear; more discussion on convexity, initialization and convergence guarantees would be helpful.
- Experimental choices limit interpretability: base classifiers are relatively simple (logistic, a "bare-bones" CNN) which is defensible because quantification targets imperfect classifiers, but it would be informative to report sensitivity to classifier strength. The omission of HDx/VA on CIFAR for feature-conversion reasons is sensible, but leaves out comparison on that dataset.
- There is little statistical analysis of results—no significance testing or confidence intervals are provided; runtimes and scalability (how these methods perform as number of classes or bins grows) are not reported.
- The paper contains some presentation issues (duplicated sentences and stray LaTeX/hyperref notes at the end) that should be cleaned up.

Suggestions for improvement:
1) Add robustness experiments that perturb feature distributions between train and test to quantify how violations of the main assumption affect performance. 2) Provide more implementation details: binning strategy, number of CV folds and repeats, solver parameters, and an analysis of computational cost and scalability. 3) Clarify properties of the Hellinger optimization (convexity, recommended solvers, and initialization strategies) and, if possible, provide guarantees or references for convergence. 4) Include statistical tests or confidence intervals for the reported MAD and accuracy numbers to support claimed differences between methods. 5) Fix presentation issues and remove duplicated text.

Verdict:
The paper makes a useful conceptual and practical contribution by unifying and extending quantification approaches to the multi-class case and by giving concrete optimization formulations. However, the paper would be stronger with deeper empirical analysis of robustness to distributional shifts, more implementation detail, and clearer discussion of optimization properties. I recommend acceptance as a poster contribution (or a short oral) after revisions addressing the points above.
